item.title: Edge AI
item.description:
Machine learning inference executed locally on industrial gateways, IPCs (Intelligent Programmable Controllers), or purpose-built edge compute devices rather than in cloud or centralized data centers.

Distinguishing Characteristics

Latency: 1–10 milliseconds for inference and decision-making (compared to 50–500ms round-trip to cloud).

Autonomy: Continues operating even if cloud connectivity is lost, with degraded but functional capabilities.

Safety: Local processing is mandatory for safety-critical decisions where even small network delays would be unacceptable. A bearing failure detection system must trigger an emergency stop in milliseconds, not seconds.

item.title: Activation Trigger
item.description:
An activation trigger is the mechanism by which an agentic system decides — without explicit user instruction — to load a skill, invoke an agent, or modify its behavior. The system matches a description field in the skill or agent’s metadata against the current conversational context. If the description matches, the skill loads. If it does not, the skill stays inert. The description is therefore load-bearing infrastructure, not documentation.

The mechanism works by semantic matching, not keyword lookup. A skill described as “Use when writing Python test files” will activate when the user asks to add tests to a Python module, even if they never say the word “skill.” The model reads the description, reads the context, and decides whether the skill is relevant. This means the quality of the description directly determines whether the skill fires at the right time, fires at the wrong time, or never fires at all.

item.title: Agentic Coding
item.description:
Agentic coding is software development in which an AI agent autonomously reads, writes, executes, and tests code — acting directly on the codebase and development environment rather than suggesting changes for a human to apply. The agent forms a plan, takes actions (editing files, running commands, reading output), observes results, and iterates. The human’s role shifts from writing code to reviewing the agent’s work and providing direction.

The distinction from code completion (autocomplete, inline suggestions) is the locus of action. Code completion predicts what a human is about to type and offers fragments; the human remains the actor. Agentic coding inverts this: the agent is the actor, the human is the reviewer. The distinction from chat-based code assistance (asking an AI to explain or generate a snippet) is that agentic coding includes execution — the agent does not just produce text, it runs it, observes the output, and responds to errors.

item.title: Context Compaction
item.description:
Context compaction is the technique of summarizing earlier messages in an agentic conversation as the session approaches the context window limit. Rather than truncating old messages (losing information) or ending the session (losing continuity), compaction replaces detailed earlier turns with condensed summaries that preserve the key decisions, findings, and state established so far.

The mechanism operates automatically. When the conversation’s token count approaches the window limit, the system identifies older turns that can be summarized without losing load-bearing information — typically early research, exploration, and intermediate reasoning — and replaces them with a compact summary. Recent turns (the current working context) remain intact. The result is that the agent retains awareness of what happened earlier in the session while freeing token budget for continued work.

item.title: Tool Call
item.description: A tool call is the atomic unit of interaction between an AI agent and its environment. The model generates a structured request — a tool name and a set of parameters conforming to a schema — and the host runtime executes the request against the environment and returns a result. The model then reasons about the result and may issue further tool calls. This call-observe-reason-call loop is the basic rhythm of agentic behavior.

Agent memory is the set of mechanisms by which an agentic system persists information across interactions. Persistent state is one of the three conditions defining an agentic system; agent memory is how that condition is satisfied in practice.

Memory types

Conversation history. The simplest form: the full sequence of messages exchanged between user and agent, maintained in the context window. This is immediate and high-fidelity but bounded — when the conversation exceeds the window, older messages must be dropped or summarized. Conversation history is volatile; it is lost when the process terminates unless explicitly persisted.

Agent orchestration is the coordination of one or more agentic systems through a defined execution topology. The topology determines which steps execute in what order, under what conditions, and with what state. Orchestration sits between the raw tool use primitive and the goal the system is pursuing.

The simplest orchestration is a linear chain: call tool A, feed the result to tool B, return. The most complex involve graph-structured workflows with conditional branching, parallel execution, checkpointing, rollback, human-in-the-loop pauses, and dynamic subgraph composition.

An agentic system is a computational system that meets three conditions. It acts on its environment through tool use — it writes files, calls APIs, modifies databases. It pursues goals through multi-step planning — it decomposes objectives into sequences of actions and selects among alternatives. It maintains state that persists across interactions — it remembers what it has done and can resume after interruption.

All three conditions are necessary. A chatbot generates text but does not act on its environment. A script calls APIs but follows a fixed path — it does not plan. A stateless tool-calling system acts and may plan but forgets between invocations. Each satisfies at most two conditions. An agentic system satisfies all three.

Coordination overhead is the token cost, latency, and error amplification incurred when multiple agents communicate and synchronize. Every message between agents consumes tokens. Every synchronization point adds latency. Every handoff introduces an opportunity for misinterpretation. Coordination overhead is the tax on multi-agent systems that single-agent systems avoid.

The overhead has three components. Token cost: agents communicate through natural language, which is expensive per unit of information transferred. Latency: coordination points require agents to wait for each other, and this grows approximately quadratically with the number of agents. Error amplification: each handoff between agents is an opportunity for misinterpretation, and errors cascade — one agent’s mistake becomes the next agent’s input.

Durable execution is an execution model where workflow state is persisted at every step so that a process interrupted by crashes, network failures, or restarts resumes from its last completed step. The workflow function looks like ordinary sequential code, but the runtime automatically checkpoints state after each step and replays completed steps on recovery without re-executing their side effects.

The concept originates in workflow orchestration systems: Azure Durable Functions, AWS Step Functions, and Temporal all implement durable execution for distributed systems. In agentic systems, durable execution solves a specific problem: agent workflows involve expensive LLM calls and irreversible tool actions (file writes, API calls, database mutations). Re-executing a failed workflow from the beginning wastes tokens on completed work, may produce different results (LLM output is stochastic), and may duplicate side effects.

Human-in-the-loop (HITL) is an agent orchestration pattern where execution pauses at designated points for human review, approval, or modification before the agent continues. The human inspects the agent’s proposed action, the current state, or an intermediate result, then approves, edits, or rejects it. Execution resumes with the human’s decision incorporated.

HITL serves two functions. First, it catches errors — hallucinated tool calls, incorrect plans, unsafe actions — before they take effect. Second, it provides a control mechanism: the human retains decision authority over actions that are irreversible, high-stakes, or outside the agent’s competence.

A multi-agent system is an architecture where two or more agentic systems coordinate to accomplish a shared goal. Each agent has its own tools, state, and reasoning process. The agents communicate through defined protocols — shared state, message passing, or inter-agent protocols like A2A — and their joint behavior produces outcomes no single agent achieves alone.

Four coordination patterns cover most multi-agent architectures:

Orchestrator-workers. A central agent decomposes a task, assigns subtasks to worker agents, collects results, and synthesizes a response. The orchestrator controls the workflow; workers execute in isolation. Claude Code’s subagent model follows this pattern.

Prompt chaining is the simplest agent orchestration pattern: a fixed sequence of LLM calls where each call’s output becomes the next call’s input. Between steps, the application may transform the output, validate it against a schema, call a tool, or inject additional context. The sequence is predefined by the developer; the LLM controls the content of each step but not the execution path.

Anthropic’s “Building Effective Agents” (December 2024) places prompt chaining at the low-autonomy end of the orchestration spectrum:

Structured output is output from a large language model that conforms to a predefined schema — a JSON Schema, a typed record, or another machine-parseable format — rather than free-form text. The model generates text that happens to be valid JSON (or XML, or YAML) because it has been fine-tuned on examples of schema-conformant output and, in some implementations, because the decoding process constrains token selection to only those tokens that maintain schema validity.

item.title: Agent Evaluation
item.description:
Agent evaluation is the practice of measuring agentic system performance. The core question is whether an agent setup produces value — completing tasks correctly, reliably, and at acceptable cost — or wastes tokens on coordination overhead, hallucinated actions, and cascading errors.

Benchmarks

SWE-bench (Jimenez et al., ICLR 2024) is the most widely cited agent benchmark. It presents 2,294 real GitHub issues from 12 Python repositories and measures whether an agent can produce a correct patch. Top scores reached ~74.4% by end of 2025. Variants include SWE-Bench Verified (human-validated subset), SWE-Bench Multilingual, and SWE-Bench Lite (300 representative problems).

item.title: Agent Observability
item.description:
Agent observability is the practice of instrumenting agentic systems to make their decisions, tool calls, state transitions, and failure modes inspectable. In traditional software, observability means logs, metrics, and traces. In agentic systems, observability must additionally capture why the model made each decision — what context it saw, what alternatives it considered, and what state it was operating on.

Observability matters more in agentic systems than in conversational LLM use because agents are stateful and errors compound. Anthropic’s engineering blog on their production multi-agent research system states this directly: “minor failures cascade into entirely different trajectories.” A wrong tool call in step 3 changes the state that step 4 operates on, which changes step 5’s plan, which produces an output unrelated to the original goal. Without tracing, diagnosing the root cause requires manually reconstructing the agent’s reasoning across every step.

item.title: Agent Self-Improvement
item.description:
Agent self-improvement is the capability of an agentic system to modify its own prompts, tools, or workflows based on evaluated performance. The agent executes a task, a grading system scores the output, and the scores feed back into the agent’s instructions or configuration for the next iteration. The loop runs: execute → evaluate → revise → execute.

The practical pattern

The OpenAI cookbook documents a three-stage production pattern:

  1. Baseline agent performs the task (e.g., content transformation, code generation, document analysis).
  2. Evaluation layer scores the output through multiple graders. Effective grading combines deterministic checks (entity preservation, output length, schema validity) with semantic evaluation (cosine similarity to reference outputs, LLM-as-judge scoring). Start with deterministic graders; add semantic evaluation only when deterministic checks are insufficient.
  3. Metaprompt agent receives the grader scores and the current instructions, then generates revised instructions that address the identified weaknesses. The revised instructions replace the originals for the next iteration.

This pattern improves agent performance on measured criteria without fine-tuning the underlying model — the model’s weights stay fixed; only its system prompt changes. The recommended starting pass threshold is ~75%, and human review remains essential before deploying revised instructions to production.

item.title: Agent-to-Agent Protocol
item.description:
The Agent-to-Agent protocol (A2A) is an open, vendor-neutral protocol enabling communication and interoperability between opaque agentic systems. It is the inter-agent complement to MCP: MCP standardizes how an agent connects to tools; A2A standardizes how agents connect to each other.

A2A was announced by Google Cloud at Google Cloud Next on April 9, 2025, with 50+ technology partners at launch. Google donated the protocol to the Linux Foundation on June 24, 2025 under the Apache 2.0 license. The Linux Foundation formally established the Agent2Agent Protocol Project on February 27, 2026, with AWS, Cisco, Google, Microsoft, Salesforce, SAP, and ServiceNow as founding contributors. Version 1.0 — the first stable, production-ready release — shipped in early 2026, with 150+ supporting organizations.

item.title: AGENTS.md
item.description:
AGENTS.md is a file convention for providing AI coding agents with project-specific instructions. It is a Markdown file placed in a project’s root directory (or subdirectories in monorepos) that functions as a README for agents — a dedicated, predictable location for context that would clutter a human-oriented README: build commands, test procedures, code style rules, git workflow conventions, and security boundaries.

The convention emerged from a collaboration across the AI development ecosystem. OpenAI introduced it alongside the Codex research preview (May 2025) and released the format publicly in August 2025. Collaborators included OpenAI (Codex), Google (Jules), Cursor, Factory, and Amp. On December 9, 2025, the Linux Foundation announced the Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), with AGENTS.md as one of its three founding project contributions alongside Anthropic’s MCP and Block’s goose.

item.title: Computer Use
item.description:
Computer use is the capability of a large language model to interact with graphical desktop environments by interpreting screen content (screenshots) and producing mouse clicks, keyboard inputs, and other GUI actions. Anthropic released computer use as a beta capability with Claude 3.5 Sonnet on October 22, 2024.

Standard tool use connects a model to APIs — structured interfaces with defined inputs and outputs. Computer use extends this to graphical interfaces that have no API. The model receives a screenshot of the current screen state, reasons about what it sees (buttons, text fields, menus, error messages), and emits actions (click at coordinates, type text, press key combinations) that the host environment executes. The cycle repeats: act, observe the new screen state, act again.

item.title: LangGraph
item.description:
LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework for building stateful, multi-step AI agent workflows. It is developed by the LangChain team (Harrison Chase et al.) and released under the MIT license. Development began in summer 2023, with an initial release in January 2024 and a v1.0 stable release on October 22, 2025, committing to no breaking changes until v2.0. The current version as of March 2026 is 1.1.0.

LangGraph’s core abstraction is a directed graph where nodes are processing steps (Python functions or LangChain runnables), edges define transitions between them (static or conditional based on state), and state is a typed object (TypedDict or Pydantic model) that persists across the graph’s execution. Every node receives the current state as input and returns state updates as output. The graph structure is the control flow; the state object is the data flow.

item.title: Model Context Protocol
item.description: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open protocol standardizing how agentic systems connect to external tools and data sources. Anthropic released MCP in November 2024. On December 9, 2025, it became a founding project of the Linux Foundation’s Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), alongside AGENTS.md and Block’s goose. As of early 2026, the ecosystem includes 5,800+ servers, 300+ clients, and 97M+ monthly SDK downloads. OpenAI, Google, Microsoft, and all major IDEs have adopted it.

item.title: PydanticAI
item.description:
PydanticAI is a Python framework for building agentic systems with type safety, structured outputs, and model-agnostic design. It is created and maintained by Samuel Colvin and the Pydantic team — the same team behind Pydantic, Python’s dominant data validation library. The framework applies Pydantic’s philosophy (define the shape of your data, let the library enforce it) to AI agent development.

PydanticAI was publicly announced on December 2, 2024. Version 1.0 was released on September 4, 2025, after nine months of iteration and 15 million downloads. The current version as of March 2026 is 1.73.0, with Production/Stable status on PyPI. MIT license.

item.title: SOUL.md
item.description:
SOUL.md is a Markdown-based convention for encoding an AI agent’s identity, personality, communication style, expertise, and operational boundaries into files that any LLM-based agent can read and embody. Where AGENTS.md provides project-level instructions (what to do and how), SOUL.md defines who the agent is — its voice, worldview, values, and behavioral constraints.

The convention was created by Aaron J. Mars (@aaronjmars) and published to GitHub around February 2, 2026. It is closely associated with the OpenClaw ecosystem but is designed to be tool-agnostic. The philosophical basis, cited in the project documentation, is The First Paradigm of Consciousness Uploading by Liu Xiaoben, which proposes that consciousness is encoded in the language one produces and that a model conditioned on a person’s lifetime language output constitutes a form of identity persistence.

item.title: Tool Use
item.description: Tool use is the mechanism by which a language model acts on its environment. The model produces structured output — a JSON object specifying a function name and arguments — and a host application executes the function. The result comes back to the model in the next conversation turn. The model decides what to call and with what arguments. The host application decides whether to execute. Neither has access to the other’s internals.

item.title: ClawHub
item.description:
ClawHub is the public skill registry for OpenClaw. It provides a discovery and distribution surface for versioned skill bundles.

Role

The official docs position ClawHub as the normal way to search, install, update, and publish OpenClaw skills. Its CLI supports search, installation, updates, and sync-based publishing.

Function

ClawHub turns skills into portable, shareable artifacts rather than only local folders. It therefore extends OpenClaw’s skill system with a public registry layer.

item.title: OpenClaw ACP Agent
item.description:
An OpenClaw ACP agent is an external harness runtime that OpenClaw can run through an ACP backend plugin. ACP agents let OpenClaw delegate work to tools such as Codex, Claude Code, OpenCode, Gemini CLI, or Pi without reducing them to native OpenClaw sub-agents.

Role

The official ACP docs distinguish ACP sessions from OpenClaw-native sub-agent runs. ACP is used when the operator wants an external runtime, its own session semantics, or harness-specific capabilities.

item.title: OpenClaw DM Policy
item.description:
The OpenClaw DM policy is the configuration that determines how OpenClaw handles inbound direct messages before they reach an agent. It is a channel-level gate on message processing, not only a cosmetic notification preference.

Modes

OpenClaw documents four DM policy modes:

  • pairing: unknown senders receive a short code and their message is ignored until approval
  • allowlist: only approved senders can reach the bot
  • open: any sender can reach the bot if the channel allowlist explicitly opts in
  • disabled: inbound DMs are ignored

Function

This policy is one of OpenClaw’s primary safety controls for consumer messaging surfaces. It constrains who can steer a tool-using assistant before prompts, tool calls, or session routing become relevant.

item.title: OpenClaw Gateway
item.description:
The OpenClaw Gateway is the long-running control-plane process in an OpenClaw deployment. It coordinates sessions, channels, tools, configuration, routing, and web interfaces while the assistant remains the user-facing product surface.

Role

The Gateway gives OpenClaw a persistent runtime. It keeps the assistant reachable through chat channels, the Control UI, WebChat, and paired device surfaces without reducing the system to a single interactive terminal session.

Trust boundary

OpenClaw’s security model treats the Gateway as a trusted operator boundary, not as a hostile multi-tenant service. Within one Gateway instance, authenticated control-plane access carries authority over the configured agents, sessions, and policies in that deployment.

item.title: OpenClaw HEARTBEAT.md
item.description:
OpenClaw HEARTBEAT.md is an optional workspace file that defines what the assistant should check or notice when it is idle. It is the main configuration surface for low-frequency proactive behavior.

Role

The official heartbeat documentation describes HEARTBEAT.md as a small checklist that the default prompt reads on a regular heartbeat. Independent guides distinguish it from cron: cron handles scheduled tasks at exact times, while HEARTBEAT.md handles opportunistic awareness and gentle proactive suggestions.

item.title: OpenClaw Hook
item.description:
An OpenClaw hook is a directory-discovered event handler that runs when command or lifecycle events fire inside the Gateway. Hooks are the event-driven automation layer of OpenClaw.

Structure

The official hooks docs describe a hook as a directory containing HOOK.md plus a TypeScript handler. Hooks are discovered from workspace, managed, and bundled directories, with workspace hooks having highest precedence.

Role

Hooks let operators attach automation to events such as /new, bootstrap, or gateway startup. The bundled session-memory, bootstrap-extra-files, command-logger, and boot-md hooks show the main current uses: memory capture, workspace augmentation, audit logging, and startup actions.

item.title: OpenClaw Memory
item.description:
OpenClaw memory is the Markdown-based memory layer in an OpenClaw workspace. Its source of truth is written files on disk rather than session-local model state.

Structure

The official memory docs describe two default durable layers:

  • MEMORY.md for curated long-term memory
  • memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md for dated logs and running context

Memory search tools are provided by the active memory plugin, but the files remain primary. Search augments retrieval; it does not replace the underlying Markdown record.

item.title: OpenClaw Node
item.description:
An OpenClaw node is a paired device surface that exposes host-local capabilities to an OpenClaw Gateway. Nodes let the assistant act through device-specific interfaces such as voice, camera, screen capture, notifications, and host-local command execution.

Pairing model

OpenClaw documents node pairing as an operator-approved connection to a Gateway. Once paired, node actions are treated as trusted operator actions on that device rather than as requests from an untrusted external sender.

item.title: OpenClaw Plugin Slot
item.description:
An OpenClaw plugin slot is an exclusive plugin category where only one plugin can own a given runtime role at a time. Slots let OpenClaw keep its core lean while making selected extension points explicit and mutually exclusive.

Examples

OpenClaw documents memory and contextEngine as plugin slots. If multiple plugins declare the same exclusive kind, only the selected plugin loads for that slot and the others remain disabled.

Architectural function

Plugin slots separate extension from aggregation. They allow OpenClaw to swap implementations for a core runtime role, such as memory or context orchestration, without loading competing implementations simultaneously.

item.title: OpenClaw Skill
item.description:
An OpenClaw skill is a directory-based instruction and tool bundle that teaches an OpenClaw agent how to perform a specialized task. The official docs define a skill as a directory containing a SKILL.md file and, optionally, scripts or other resources.

Loading model

The official skills documentation describes three main loading locations:

  • bundled skills shipped with OpenClaw
  • managed or local skills under ~/.openclaw/skills
  • workspace skills under <workspace>/skills

When names conflict, workspace skills take precedence over shared and bundled skills.

item.title: OpenClaw SOUL.md
item.description:
OpenClaw SOUL.md is a workspace file that defines the assistant’s identity, style, values, and behavioral boundaries. In practice it is the main place where an operator specifies how the assistant should speak, what it should prioritize, and where it should stop.

Role

The official bootstrapping flow writes SOUL.md during first-run setup after collecting identity and preference information. Independent setup guides treat it as the highest-leverage file to write early because it shapes every later interaction.

item.title: OpenProse
item.description:
OpenProse is a markdown-first workflow format and OpenClaw plugin for explicit, reusable agent programs. In OpenClaw it ships as a plugin that installs a skill pack plus a /prose slash command.

Role

Programs live in .prose files and can spawn multiple sub-agents with explicit control flow. The official docs describe research and synthesis, incident triage, code review, and content pipelines as core use cases.

Scope

OpenProse sits above individual tools and below general agent behavior. It is the program layer for repeatable multi-step workflows.

item.title: QMD
item.description:
QMD is a local-first search sidecar that OpenClaw can use as an experimental memory backend. In this mode, Markdown remains the source of truth while OpenClaw shells out to QMD for retrieval.

Role

The official memory docs describe QMD as combining BM25, vectors, and reranking over local Markdown-backed memory. OpenClaw can opt into it with memory.backend = "qmd".

Scope

QMD is not OpenClaw’s canonical memory store. It is a retrieval layer that sits on top of files such as MEMORY.md and memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md to improve search and recall.

item.title: Inference as a Tool
item.description:
When a work chain needs “inference” (reasoning, summarization, planning), treat it as a tool with a well-specified contract rather than an implicit side-effect.

  • Invocation kinds: prefer runtime.invocation: inference (LLM/human) alongside runtime.invocation: manual for human-only steps. Use entrypoint: none when no script exists and the action is conversational.
  • Skill shape: define inputs/outputs so tool calls can be validated and logged. For LLM-backed calls, accept messages + context and return text plus any structured fields the chain needs.
  • Routing: callers supply credentials via headers (e.g., X-OpenAI-Key) or session context; skills should not embed provider keys.
  • Audit: log tool calls and results as skill executions, even for inference-only steps, to keep chains observable.
  • Fallbacks: allow manual/human fulfillment by setting invocation: manual and recording the resulting text in the same output schema.

This keeps inference interchangeable (LLM or human), observable, and composable with other skills in an agential semioverse.
